Looks nice but I really wish we could see more WWI and WWII movies seen from a German perspective. In particular with WWII the German experience was profoundly different from the British. What we think of as typical WWI with totally incompetent commanders sending sending hordes of people into machine gun fire and really muddy trenches, but this was primarily the British experience.

Germans e.g. had far better built trenches with sleeping quarters. They also used experienced commanders who advanced in the ranks not inexperienced upperclass morons to lead the troops.

But for Germany the psychology was quite different. It developed into a deeply unpopular war which people no longer wanted. German soldiers also have significant problems with low food rations and low morale towards the end.

I think all of these perspective could have made for a really interesting WWI movie.
Erik Engheim the fact that they are British has almost zero effect on the story

@nmanswager Perhaps his point is that the tendency to issue nonsensical orders is a theme which is frquently present in movies portraying the British experience, because it was a real problem that was less relevant on the German side. I think he's right, a film which showed how the war began, progressed and changed over time and finally ended for German soldiers and ordinary Germans would be interesting, especially in what it setup for the future via the terrible Treaty of Versaille, how the Great Depression influenced events in Germany, producing fertile ground for the rise of nazism. Those who fail to learn from history are doomed to repeat it, yet there's been very little cultural effort post-WW2 to do this, especially of WW2, and ironically very much the case within Germany itself where even basic visual aspects of the latter conflict are taboo. Instead, the entire period has been used a a guilt bashing stick to wallop the modern German populace into collective demographic and cultural suicide, a mass sins-of-the-fathers brainwashing (don't talk about it, but at the same time just accept Germany bad, Allies good, now open those borders and swallow the Merkel guilt pill).


I'm not saying we need movies which attempt to portray the German experience in a deliberately positive-slanted manner, that would be equally daft, but at the moment the entire cinematic narrative of both wars is very one-sided. Modern cinema has a tendency to focus mainly on the spectacle of the what in history, but I think the essence of the why is more interesting and useful, it just needs a production team that knows how to convey a story well and a studio willing to fund it. Funny how decades ago there were actually more movies that looked at such things, such as "The Iron Cross" (museum exhibits I've seen which explain what some I.C.s were awarded for are fascinating, yet today in cinema even the notion that there were "brave" Germans during either war is rarely depicted).


Recommended movie and reading references:


https://www.amazon.co.uk/gp/video/detail/B01GEDGTIS/

https://www.amazon.co.uk/gp/product/1539586391/
https://www.amazon.co.uk/gp/product/1909384534/


I have hundreds of documentaries, etc. about both wars, though mainly the latter. The more one learns, the more one realises that Hollywood isn't capable of conveying the big picture, because real history, what actually happened and why, is often too uncomfortable, but the hard questions ought to be asked. Instead, cinema and popular media in general has encouraged the public to hold a very simplistic view of things, whether for example that's ignoring the questionable morality of the nature of the Allied bombing campaign towards the end of WW2 (has there ever been a movie about Bomber Harris? If the Allies had lost WW2, Harris would have been tried for war crimes for sure), or why the attacks on Hiroshima and Nagasaki were not only militarily sensible from the perspective of the time - saving some 250K US lives - but also likely saved several million Japanese lives (that is, compared to a conventional land invasion) and quite possibly helped prevent the cold war from turning hot (also ensured Russia didn't take over China after the highly successful invasion of Manchuria).


Or what about the conventional bombing campaigns over Japan? The larger attacks on Tokyo killed far more people than Little Boy and Fatman combined (estimates vary, but perhaps as many as a million), yet there's little awareness of these events in the minds of the modern public. A curious thing btw, after WW2 ended both the Allies and the remnants of the Japanese authorities (for different reasons) did not want the scale of the destruction to be made known to the public. A great deal has been written and said of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, but almost nothing of the more lethal attacks on Tokyo and elsewhere; the blind notion of nuclear=bad has been too useful for purposes of entertainment and political messaging for decades. Look up, "Operation Meetinghouse" (9th March 1945); Hollywood will never make a movie about the events of that particular night in Japan.
Too dicey to give the German perspective. Nazis and all that. I think you may have a point but no one will touch that with a 10 foot pole. Simple as that

@mapesdhs597 What absolutely intelligent (and rare) commentary... you really know your subject, and it shows.

I'm a huge fan of history, WWII in particular. I grew up with excellent films/series from the American perspective, such as "The Memphis Belle", "Saving Private Ryan", "Band of Brothers", where Germans were mostly just faceless enemies throwing rounds from offscreen... great stories, but lacking total context and therefore the complete truth.

If I may make another recommendation to anyone reading:  "A Higher Call" by Adam Makos, centered around the Charlie Brown/Franz Stigler incident. The book ended up being mostly about Stigler's experience in the Luftwaffe... to say it was transformative is an understatement. It changed everything I thought about German pilots in WWII, it even changed what I thought about our own airmen in the European theater, and further changed how I approached all of America's enemies in WWII and throughout history. That's the impact of a complete perspective, and the book was that good.

Unfortunately, don't expect Hollywood to produce anything remotely "unsafe". They won't go anywhere that blows against the winds of cultural sensitivity/political ideology... and if anything this trend is getting worse, not better. Reality is simply too "dangerous" for a couple of hours of escapism.
read 'Forgotten Victory' by Gary Sheffield for a concise overview from the British perspective.

"...But the reality of shaving in the trenches of World War 1 was much more difficult than you can imagine. Many soldiers initially carried razors as part of their standard kit. However the wet and muddy conditions of the Western Front meant that they soon became rusty and unusable. The lack of sharpening stones or strops also meant that razor blades became blunt quickly. By 1915 razors became scare on the front lines. There are records and letters from front line soldiers requesting socks, chocolates and razors from their loved ones back home."

All sorts of Oscar buzz on THIS one.   Here it is Christmas day, and I was looking online to see where it would be opening in Reno.    At first glance, I see it'll be playing in one theater somewhere downtown......but meanwhile, the BIG multi-screen theaters are committed to plastering MOST of their screens with that Star Wars POS.     And Cats.     I wonder how many chains are locked into contracts, where they HAVE to carry those other films.....and for how long.     I'm sure some fast-food franchise paid big money to have images from those other films all over their cups, etc.    LOL.

Re locked into contracts, you're right. In comments for another video one guy wrote of his local cinema where he's friends with the manager. The manager told him the cinema chain has a viewing deal with the distributor or somesuch, they have to sell a certain number of tickets, etc. Attendence for ROS was extremely low, so he was worried how he could get more people to come and see the movie. Interesting isn't it? I mean the cinema owner knowing it's a bad film, but in a position of having to try and persuade the public to see it because that's the deal to which they've signed up, in which case the nature of the PR for such a film isn't remotely organic, its apparent popularity isn't derived purely from viewer opinion; it'll always be distorted overly favourably, and the DIsney machine has the power to ram this through. I wonder what the consequences are for cinemas, especially independents, who refuse to show bad movies - blocked from future contracts, etc. I expect. Pity nobody has done an expose of how this industry works because I bet behind the scenes it really stinks.
